questions.question: Money paid for service is
questions.solution: An 'Honorarium' is a payment given for professional services rendered voluntarily or for which fees are not legally required (Latin: honorarium = relating to honor). 'Fee' = a payment for professional advice; 'Remuneration' = payment for work done; 'Wage' = regular payment by employer. Students confuse Honorarium with Fee — Honorarium is given as an honor/courtesy for services not legally requiring payment, Fee is the standard charge for a professional service.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money to old or sick non-workers is
questions.solution: A 'Pension' is a regular payment made to retired, elderly, or disabled persons (Latin: pensio = a payment). 'Subsidy' = money given by government to support a business; 'Benefit' = a payment to which someone is entitled; 'Allowance' = money given for a specific purpose. Students confuse Pension with Benefit — Pension is specifically for retired/aged/disabled non-workers, Benefit is a broader government payment for various needs.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money for help in dishonest deal is
questions.solution: A 'Kickback' is a payment made to someone for facilitating a transaction or appointment, especially as part of a corrupt arrangement (kick + back = returning part of payment). 'Bribe' = money to influence judgment; 'Hush money' = paid to ensure silence; 'Payoff' = a bribe or financial benefit. Students confuse Kickback with Bribe — Kickback specifically involves a return payment to one who facilitated a deal, Bribe is paid upfront to corrupt a decision.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money paid at one time is a
questions.solution: A 'Lump sum' is a single payment made at one time rather than in installments (lump = compact mass + sum = amount). 'Installment' = one of several payments; 'Annuity' = a fixed annual payment; 'Down payment' = initial partial payment. Students confuse Lump sum with Down payment — Down payment is a partial first payment with more to follow, Lump sum is the complete amount paid at once.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money spent is an
questions.solution: 'Expenditure' is the action of spending funds; money spent (Latin: expendere = to weigh out/pay). 'Disbursement' = paying out money from a fund; 'Outlay' = an amount of money spent; 'Cost' = the amount paid to acquire something. Students confuse Expenditure with Outlay — Expenditure is the general comprehensive term for money spent, Outlay implies a specific capital spending for investment/setup.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Money collected by authority is a
questions.solution: A 'Levy' is a tax or other compulsory payment collected by an authority (French: levée = raising). 'Tax' = compulsory payment to government; 'Toll' = payment to use a road/bridge; 'Fine' = payment as a penalty. Students confuse Levy with Tax — Levy is the act of collecting or the general term for compulsory collection, Tax is the specific government-imposed charge on income/goods.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Amount produced is
questions.solution: 'Yield' is the amount of something produced (especially agricultural or industrial output) (Old English: gieldan = to pay). 'Output' = general term for production; 'Produce' = fresh food/agricultural products; 'Product' = something manufactured. Students confuse Yield with Output — Yield specifically refers to the amount produced relative to input (especially agriculture/investment), Output is the general quantity produced.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Time holding job or title is
questions.solution: 'Tenure' is the holding of an office, the conditions under which it is held, or the period of such holding (Latin: tenere = to hold). 'Incumbency' = the holding of an office or position; 'Term' = a fixed period; 'Duration' = the time something lasts. Students confuse Tenure with Incumbency — Tenure refers to the holding conditions and period of any position, Incumbency specifically refers to a current holder's period in office.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Amusing and very strange is
questions.solution: 'Wacky' means amusing and very strange or unusual in behavior (origin uncertain, possibly from 'whack'). 'Bizarre' = very strange or unusual; 'Eccentric' = unconventional in behavior; 'Whimsical' = playfully quaint. Students confuse Wacky with Bizarre — Bizarre implies strangeness that may be unsettling, Wacky implies strangeness that is amusing and entertaining.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Angry unpleasant woman is a
questions.solution: A 'Shrew' is a bad-tempered or aggressively assertive woman (from Shakespeare's 'The Taming of the Shrew'; originally a small aggressive mammal). 'Harpy' = a grasping, unpleasant woman; 'Termagant' = a harsh-tempered woman; 'Vixen' = a female fox, or a quarrelsome woman. Students find this difficult as all four refer to unpleasant women — Shrew specifically emphasizes bad-temperedness and scolding nature.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Complicated long argument is a
questions.solution: A 'Wrangle' is a long, complicated dispute or argument (Low German: wrangeln = to wrestle). 'Dispute' = a disagreement; 'Altercation' = a noisy argument; 'Quarrel' = an angry disagreement. Students confuse Wrangle with Altercation — Altercation implies a noisy public confrontation, Wrangle implies a prolonged, complicated, and exhausting argument.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Invigilator at university exam is a
questions.solution: A 'Proctor' is a person who invigilates (supervises) students during an examination, especially at a university (Latin: procurator = a person acting for another). 'Invigilator' = supervisor during exam; 'Warden' = a guardian/official. Students confuse Proctor with Invigilator — both supervise exams, but Proctor is the specific university/formal term, Invigilator is the more general British English term.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Ancient text like book of medicines is a
questions.solution: A 'Codex' is an ancient manuscript text, especially in book form (Latin: codex = tree trunk/wooden tablet, later a bound book). 'Manuscript' = a handwritten text; 'Scroll' = a roll of papyrus/parchment; 'Tome' = a large heavy book. Students confuse Codex with Manuscript — a Manuscript is any handwritten document, a Codex is specifically an ancient book-form manuscript with bound pages as opposed to a scroll.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If I am (a) the principal of the school (b) I would abolish all examinations. (c) No error (d)
questions.solution: 'If I am' is incorrect in a hypothetical conditional; the correct form is 'If I were'. The sentence uses a subjunctive mood requiring 'were'. Students often confuse simple present with subjunctive in wish/hypothetical sentences.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: My mother is thinking (a) that I should (b) stop drinking. (c) No error. (d)
questions.solution: 'Is thinking' (present continuous) is incorrect for a permanent mental state; 'thinks' (simple present) is correct. Stative verbs like 'think', 'believe', 'know' are not used in continuous tenses. Students frequently use continuous tense with stative verbs.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: LIC was found (a) on September 1, 1956 (b) via the Life Insurance Corporation Act. (c) No error. (d)
questions.optionC: via the Life Insurance Corporation Act.
questions.solution: 'Was found' is incorrect; the correct word is 'was founded' (established). 'Found' means to discover, while 'founded' means to establish an organisation. Confusion between 'find/found' and 'found/founded' is very common.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Full many a flower (a) are born (b) to blush unseen. (c) No error. (d)
questions.solution: 'Many a + singular noun' takes a singular verb; so 'are born' should be 'is born'. The phrase 'many a' always governs a singular verb. Students assume 'many' always takes a plural verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I shall be glad to help (a) every one of my boys (b) in their studies. (c) No error. (d)
questions.optionA: I shall be glad to help
questions.solution: 'Every one' is singular and requires singular pronoun 'his', not 'their'. 'Their' is plural and does not agree with 'every one'. Students often use 'their' as a neutral singular pronoun incorrectly in formal contexts.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Multiple research studies (a) have laid emphasis on the importance (b) of walking to improved glucose control. (c) No error. (d)
questions.optionB: have laid emphasis on the importance
questions.optionC: of walking to improved glucose control.
questions.solution: 'To improved' is grammatically wrong; it should be 'for improving' or 'in improving'. After 'importance of', a gerund form is required. Students confuse infinitive and gerund in prepositional phrases.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: As a screen writer, (a) there are, broadly speaking, (b) two kind of jobs in the film world. (c) No error. (d)
questions.optionC: two kind of jobs in the film world.
questions.solution: 'Two kind' is wrong; 'kind' should be plural 'kinds' after the numeral 'two'. Countable nouns must be pluralised after numbers greater than one. Students often forget to pluralise the noun after quantifiers.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If the scheme would have been implemented effectively (a) all affected (b) would have benefitted (c) No error (d)
questions.optionA: If the scheme would have been implemented effectively
questions.solution: In third conditional sentences, the 'if' clause uses past perfect ('had been'), not 'would have been'. Using 'would' in the if-clause is a common error. The correct form is 'If the scheme had been implemented effectively'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The fascination with gold at least (a) seems to be a case were traditional belief and (b) modern finance would point the same way (c) No error (d)
questions.optionA: The fascination with gold at least
questions.optionB: seems to be a case were traditional belief and
questions.optionC: modern finance would point the same way
questions.solution: 'Were' in part (b) should be 'where' — a relative adverb introducing a defining clause. Homophones 'were' (past of be) and 'where' (location/relative adverb) are commonly confused. The sentence describes a situation where two things align.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: If I were you (a) I would not go for (b) change of job (c) No error (d)
questions.solution: 'Change of job' is incorrect; it should be 'a change of job' since a countable noun needs an article. The article 'a' is missing before 'change'. Students often omit articles before countable singular nouns.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The conclusions that they came to are highly questionable (underlined: questionable).
questions.solution: 'Questionable' modifies the noun 'conclusions', making it an adjective. Adjectives describe or qualify nouns. Students may confuse it with an adverb since it ends in '-able', but it modifies a noun, not a verb.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: This year marks the quincentenary of Columbus's voyage to America (underlined: quincentenary).
questions.solution: 'Quincentenary' (500th anniversary) is used as a noun — the object of the verb 'marks'. It names a concept. Students might confuse it with an adjective if unfamiliar with the word.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I still cherish the memories of those far-off days (underlined: far-off).
questions.solution: 'Far-off' is a compound adjective modifying the noun 'days'. Adjectives can be hyphenated compound words placed before nouns. Students may think it is an adverb because 'far' can function adverbially on its own.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: I don't think I'll come on Saturday. I have a lot of work to do. Besides, I don't really like parties (underlined: Besides).
questions.solution: 'Besides' here modifies the entire clause (adds another reason), functioning as a conjunctive adverb. When 'besides' starts a sentence as a linking word, it is an adverb, not a preposition. Students confuse it with the preposition 'besides' (in addition to).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The bottom line is that we have to make a decision today (underlined: bottom line).
questions.solution: 'Bottom line' is an idiomatic noun phrase serving as the subject of the sentence. Noun phrases function as nouns. Students might think 'bottom' is an adjective describing 'line', but the whole idiom functions as a noun.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He wanted that I left immediately.
questions.solution: After 'want', the pattern is 'want + object + infinitive' (He wanted me to leave). The construction 'want that + clause' is non-standard in English. Students use the indirect speech pattern 'that + subject + verb' incorrectly with 'want'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: It was the mother of the girl of whose voice I had recognised.
questions.solution: The correct phrasing is 'the girl whose voice I had recognised' — 'of whose voice' is redundant with 'of the girl'. 'Whose' already shows possession. Students add an extra preposition when using 'whose'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The maid was laying the table for dinner.
questions.solution: 'Laying the table' is the correct idiomatic expression meaning to arrange dishes on a table for a meal. 'Lay' (transitive) takes an object, which 'the table' provides. Students confuse 'lay' with 'lie' (intransitive).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Hardly have we got into the forest when it began to rain.
questions.solution: With 'hardly...when', the verb in the main clause uses past perfect ('had hardly got') and the second clause uses simple past. When 'hardly' is at the beginning with inversion, 'Hardly had we got' is used. 'We had hardly got' restores normal order correctly.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: There is no chance of success unless you do not work hard.
questions.solution: 'Unless' already contains a negative meaning ('if not'), so adding 'do not' creates a double negative. The correct form is 'unless you work hard'. Students incorrectly add negation after 'unless'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Have you ever saw the flower of a pumpkin plant?
questions.solution: With 'have you ever', the past participle 'seen' is required to form present perfect tense. 'Saw' is simple past and cannot follow the auxiliary 'have'. Students use simple past instead of past participle after 'have/has'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Since we were knowing the correct route, we did not worry at all.
questions.solution: 'Know' is a stative verb and cannot be used in continuous tense; 'knew' (simple past) is correct as the context is past. Stative verbs like 'know', 'believe', 'understand' do not take continuous forms.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Our country can progress when only people work hard.
questions.solution: 'Only' should modify the time clause 'when people work hard', so it must precede 'when'. Misplacing 'only' changes meaning. 'Only when' correctly restricts the condition for progress.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Wake me up when father will come.
questions.solution: In time clauses introduced by 'when', simple present is used instead of future tense. 'Will come' is incorrect in a time clause; 'comes' is correct. Students import the future tense from Hindi/regional language patterns.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Do take an umbrella with you lest you do not get wet.
questions.optionB: lest you should not get wet
questions.optionC: lest you might not get wet
questions.solution: 'Lest' already means 'for fear that' (negative), so 'should' (not 'not') follows it. 'Lest you should get wet' is correct. Adding 'not' after 'lest' creates a double negative.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Select the sentence that uses 'Alibi' correctly.
questions.optionA: The accused was acquitted because he provided an alibi.
questions.optionB: The accused was acquitted because his alibi was not accepted.
questions.optionC: The accused was acquitted because his alibi was false.
questions.optionD: The accused was convicted because he provided an alibi.
questions.solution: An 'alibi' is evidence proving someone was elsewhere when a crime occurred, and it leads to acquittal. Only option (a) logically connects providing an alibi with acquittal. Options (b) and (c) contradict the meaning of alibi.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Select the sentence that uses 'Continuously' correctly.
questions.optionA: The waves lapped upon the shores continuously.
questions.optionB: The fellows at the academy follow their exercise routine continuously.
questions.optionC: The giant piston at the oil rig hammered the ground continuously.
questions.optionD: The water was flowing from the leaking tap continuously.
questions.solution: 'Continuously' means without interruption. A piston hammering the ground is an uninterrupted, repetitive mechanical action — correct usage. A routine (option b) has breaks; a tap dripping (option d) may not be truly uninterrupted. Mechanical pistons operate in true continuous strokes.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Select the sentence that uses 'Elicit' correctly.
questions.optionA: The call centre's operations were found to be elicit.
questions.optionB: The elicit ties between them were discovered by chance.
questions.optionC: The demand for further information did not elicit an enthusiastic response.
questions.optionD: The elicit reasons stated were found to be insufficient.
questions.solution: 'Elicit' means to draw out or evoke a response; option (c) uses it correctly. Options (a), (b), (d) confuse 'elicit' with 'illicit' (illegal/forbidden). This is a classic homophone confusion error.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Select the sentence that uses 'Climactic' correctly.
questions.optionA: The climactic degradations shall lead to cataclysmic weather change.
questions.optionB: The climactic changes in the environment will impact the entire world.
questions.optionC: The climactic outcomes of restrained sustainability are positive.
questions.optionD: The climactic changes presaging environmental disaster are predicted to unfold soon.
questions.solution: 'Climactic' means relating to a climax or culminating point; option (d) uses it to describe changes that are building up to a major event. Options (a)–(c) confuse 'climactic' with 'climatic' (relating to climate). This is a very common vocabulary error.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Select the sentence that uses 'Affect' correctly.
questions.optionA: The new diet did not seem to have any affect on her health.
questions.optionB: For the new scheme to take affect, certain conditions have to be met.
questions.optionC: In affect, we are back to where we were before.
questions.optionD: The changed circumstances seemed to visibly affect her.
questions.solution: 'Affect' is a verb meaning to influence or have an impact on; option (d) uses it correctly as a verb. Options (a), (b), (c) incorrectly use 'affect' in place of the noun 'effect'. The affect/effect confusion is one of the most common English errors.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The captain said to his soldiers, "Move forward and face the target now."
questions.optionA: The captain ordered his soldiers to move forward and face the target.
questions.optionB: The captain informed his soldiers that they should move forward and face the target now.
questions.optionC: The captain asked his soldiers to move forward and face the target then.
questions.optionD: The captain told his soldiers that they move forward and face the target immediately.
questions.solution: An imperative command in direct speech becomes 'ordered/commanded + object + infinitive' in indirect speech. 'Now' is dropped in reported speech. Option (a) correctly uses 'ordered' and the infinitive without 'now'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Vivek said to his friend, "Could you please turn off the switch?"
questions.optionA: Vivek told his friend to turn off the switch.
questions.optionB: Vivek asked his friend to please turn off the switch.
questions.optionC: Vivek requested his friend to turn off the switch.
questions.optionD: Vivek told his friend that he should turn off the switch.
questions.solution: 'Could you please...' is a polite request, reported as 'requested + object + infinitive'. 'Requested' correctly conveys the polite tone. 'Told' (option a/d) implies an instruction, not a request.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The manager said to his colleagues, "We have received a serious threat to our business now and we need to act to face it."
questions.optionA: The manager told his colleagues that they had received a serious threat to our business then and they needed to act to face it.
questions.optionB: The manager told his colleagues that they received a serious threat to their business then and they needed to act to face it.
questions.optionC: The manager said his colleagues that they had received a serious threat to our business then and they needed to act to face it.
questions.optionD: The manager told his colleagues that they had received a serious threat to their business at that time and they needed to act to face it.
questions.solution: In reported speech: 'said to' → 'told', present perfect → past perfect ('had received'), 'our' → 'their', 'now' → 'at that time'. Option (d) correctly applies all these transformations. Students often forget to change 'our' to 'their' or 'now' to 'then'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Romila said to Rahim, "Where were your ideas when we faced the troubles last week?"
questions.optionA: Romila asked Rahim where his ideas had been when they had faced the trouble the week before.
questions.optionB: Romila asked Rahim where his ideas had been when they faced the trouble the last week.
questions.optionC: Romila requested Rahim where his ideas had been when they faced the trouble the week before.
questions.optionD: Romila told Rahim where his ideas were when they faced the trouble the week before.
questions.solution: Wh-question in direct speech → indirect speech with 'asked + wh-word + subject + verb'; tense shifts from past to past perfect ('had been', 'had faced'); 'last week' → 'the week before'. Option (a) correctly applies all rules.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The actor said to his co-star, Sarita, "Will you go with me for a cup of tea in the evening today?"
questions.optionA: The actor said to his co-star if she would go for a cup of tea with him in evening today.
questions.optionB: The actor told his co-star, Sarita if she would go with him for a cup of tea in evening that day.
questions.optionC: The actor requests his co-star, Sarita if she would go with him for a cup of tea in that evening that day.
questions.optionD: The actor asked his co-star Sarita if she would go with him for a cup of tea in the evening that day.
questions.solution: Yes/No question reported with 'asked...if'; 'will' → 'would'; 'today' → 'that day'; article 'the' before 'evening' retained. Option (d) is complete and accurate. Students forget to retain articles or change 'today' to 'that day'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: All the developments that took place in the 20th century have had implications on the next century (underlined: implications).
questions.solution: 'Implications' means consequences or effects that follow from something. 'Consequences' is the closest synonym. 'Interferences' and 'feedback' carry different meanings. Students might choose 'feedback' by misreading context.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He is such a leader that his actions are contagious (underlined: contagious).
questions.solution: 'Contagious' means spreading from one person to another; 'transmittable' is its closest synonym. In context, the leader's actions spread/inspire others. Students may choose 'effective' by inferring context rather than focusing on word meaning.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The budget incorporated a number of tax reforms which included higher taxes for the very rich (underlined: incorporated).
questions.solution: 'Incorporated' means included or integrated as part of a whole; 'integrated' is the best synonym. 'Excluded' is the antonym. 'Laid down' means established rules, not the same.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: His thesis makes all generic statements which have already been proved (underlined: generic).
questions.solution: 'Generic' means general or broad, applicable to a whole class. 'Broad' is the best synonym here. 'Specific' is the antonym. Students may confuse 'generic' with 'crude' or 'non-standard'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: The captain produced yet another stellar show to make her team enter the semi-finals (underlined: stellar).
questions.solution: 'Stellar' means outstanding or exceptional (literally: of or relating to stars). 'Extraordinary' is the best synonym. 'Eclipse' means to overshadow. Students might choose 'eclipse' due to the astronomical connection with 'stellar'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Early medieval period was not a period of urban decay as claimed by some (underlined: decay).
questions.solution: The antonym of 'decay' (deterioration/decline) is 'survival' or 'growth'. 'Survival' best represents the opposite state. Options (b), (c), (d) are synonyms or related words to 'decay'.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: He speaks eloquently and can pull crowds (underlined: eloquently).
questions.solution: 'Eloquently' means speaking fluently and persuasively; its antonym is 'confusingly' (unclear, difficult to understand). Options (b), (c), (d) are near-synonyms of 'eloquently'. Students may choose 'powerfully' thinking of contrast with weak speech.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Everyone has to fight the inertia in the system (underlined: inertia).
questions.solution: 'Inertia' means inactivity or resistance to change; its antonym is 'activity'. Options (a), (b), (d) are all synonyms of inertia. Students must recognise that sluggishness/indolence/torpor all mean the same as inertia.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: There is a need to promote philanthropy in education (underlined: philanthropy).
questions.solution: 'Philanthropy' means love of humanity and charitable giving; its antonym is 'nastiness' (malice/ill will). Options (a) and (b) are synonyms of philanthropy. Students may overlook 'nastiness' as too informal.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: What we lack in the current times is compassion (underlined: compassion).
questions.solution: 'Compassion' means sympathy and concern for others; its antonym is 'indifference' (lack of concern). 'Empathy' is a synonym. Students may choose 'hardship' thinking of suffering, but it is not the opposite of compassion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rearrange P, Q, R, S: P=as soon as / Q=the government was pressed to do / R=something for the poor / S=the session commenced
questions.solution: The logical sequence is: 'As soon as the session commenced, the government was pressed to do something for the poor' → PSQR. Wait — reviewing: P=as soon as, S=the session commenced, Q=the government was pressed to do, R=something for the poor → PSQR. But answer key says SQRP needs review: SQRP = the session commenced / as soon as / something for the poor / the government was pressed to do — that doesn't work. Correct logical order: P(as soon as)+S(session commenced)+Q(pressed to do)+R(something for poor) = PSQR (option c).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rearrange P, Q, R, S: P=they do not come within my subject / Q=lightly esteemed by me / R=if I don't speak to them, it's because / S=and not because they are
questions.solution: Logical sequence: 'If I don't speak to them, it's because they do not come within my subject and not because they are lightly esteemed by me' → R+P+S+Q = RPSQ (option d). Students must find the dependent clause introduced by 'if' and then its explanation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rearrange P, Q, R, S: P=no magnanimous victor / Q=the fortune of war had / R=put in his power so cruelly / S=would treat those whom
questions.solution: Logical sequence: 'No magnanimous victor would treat those whom the fortune of war had put in his power so cruelly' → P+S+Q+R = PSQR. Reviewing SQRP: 'would treat those whom / no magnanimous victor / put in his power so cruelly / the fortune of war had' — doesn't flow. Correct: PSQR (option b).

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rearrange P, Q, R, S: P=call in question / Q=whatever else one may say of him / R=no one dare / S=his honesty of purpose
questions.solution: Logical sequence: 'Whatever else one may say of him, no one dare call in question his honesty of purpose' → Q+R+P+S = QRPS (option c). 'Whatever' introduces the concession clause, followed by the main assertion.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: Rearrange P, Q, R, S: P=has become / Q=ever since COVID-19 pandemic began / R=a fixture of everyday life / S=calibrating disease-related risk
questions.solution: Logical sequence: 'Ever since COVID-19 pandemic began, calibrating disease-related risk has become a fixture of everyday life' → Q+S+P+R = QSPR (option a). The time clause 'ever since' begins the sentence followed by the subject and predicate.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Where was India's first mobile tempered glass factory inaugurated in 2025?
questions.solution: India's first mobile tempered glass factory was inaugurated in Noida in 2025, marking a significant step toward self-reliance in mobile component manufacturing. This falls under the Make in India initiative. Students from Pune may instinctively choose option (a).

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Which tech company has partnered with the International Cricket Council (ICC) to promote women's cricket globally?
questions.solution: Google partnered with the ICC to promote women's cricket globally, as announced in 2024-25. This partnership focuses on digital outreach and increasing viewership. Students may confuse this with other ICC sponsorships.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Which country has been ranked as Asia's safest in the 2025 Global Peace Index?
questions.solution: Singapore consistently ranks as Asia's safest country in the Global Peace Index due to low crime rates, political stability, and strong governance. Japan is also a contender, making this a tricky question for students.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Who has been appointed as the Managing Director and CEO of Tata Motors effective October 1, 2025?
questions.solution: Girish Wagh was appointed as the MD and CEO of Tata Motors effective October 1, 2025, succeeding Shailesh Chandra. P.B. Balaji is the CFO of Tata Motors. Students may confuse the various leadership roles within Tata Motors.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Who has been appointed as Hyundai India's first Indian Managing Director and CEO effective January 1, 2026?
questions.solution: Tarun Garg became Hyundai India's first Indian MD and CEO, a historic milestone for the company. He had been serving as COO before this appointment. Students may confuse him with Shailesh Chandra who is associated with Tata Motors.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Who has been reappointed as the Attorney General of India for a second term starting October 1, 2025?
questions.solution: R. Venkataramani was reappointed as the Attorney General of India for a second term from October 2025. K.K. Venugopal held the position before him. Students often confuse the AG with the Solicitor General of India.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Which state is home to Gokul Jalashaya in Buxar and Udaipur Jheel in West Champaran, recently designated as Ramsar Wetlands?
questions.solution: Both Gokul Jalashaya (Buxar district) and Udaipur Jheel (West Champaran) are in Bihar. Buxar and West Champaran are well-known districts of Bihar. Students may confuse West Champaran with Champaran in UP.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Who has taken charge as the 47th Vice Chief of the Naval Staff?
questions.solution: Vice Admiral CR Praveen Nair assumed charge as the 47th Vice Chief of the Naval Staff. Defence appointments are frequently asked in competitive exams. Students need to track naval appointments specifically.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: Who are the primary beneficiaries of the 'Apna Ghar' resting facilities launched by the government?
questions.solution: The 'Apna Ghar' scheme provides resting facilities for truck drivers on highways to reduce fatigue-related accidents. This is part of road safety initiatives. Students may confuse it with housing schemes.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: In which Indian state has National Waterway-57 on the Kopili River been operationalised under the Maritime India Vision 2030?
questions.solution: The Kopili River flows through Assam (and partly Meghalaya), and NW-57 was operationalised in Assam under Maritime India Vision 2030. Students may confuse it with other northeastern waterways.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: Article 352 of the Indian Constitution deals with
questions.solution: Article 352 deals with the proclamation of National Emergency on grounds of war, external aggression, or armed rebellion. Article 356 deals with State Emergency (President's Rule). Students often confuse Articles 352, 356, and 360.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: The Chairman of the National Human Rights Commission is appointed by
questions.solution: The Chairman of the NHRC is appointed by the President of India on the recommendation of a committee headed by the Prime Minister. The appointment is formal and by the President. Students confuse the recommending body with the appointing authority.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: In India the power of 'Pardon' has been given to the
questions.solution: Under Article 72, the President of India has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment. Governors have similar power under Article 161 for state offences. Students sometimes attribute this power to the PM.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: The authorization for the withdrawal of funds from the Consolidated Fund of India must come from:
questions.solution: Under Article 266, withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund of India requires appropriation made by Parliament through the Appropriation Act. No money can be withdrawn without legislative sanction. Students confuse the President's role in giving assent with the authorisation for withdrawal.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: With reference to the Delimitation Commission, which statements are correct? 1. Orders cannot be challenged in Court. 2. Parliament/Assembly cannot modify the orders.
questions.solution: Both statements are correct: Delimitation Commission orders are final and cannot be questioned in any court (Statement 1), and when laid before the legislature, they cannot be modified (Statement 2). This is enshrined in Article 329. Students doubt Statement 2 thinking Parliament is supreme.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: The distribution of powers between the Centre and the States in the Indian Constitution is based on the scheme provided in the
questions.solution: The three-list system (Union, State, Concurrent) for division of powers was first introduced by the Government of India Act 1935 and adopted by the Indian Constitution. The 1919 Act introduced dyarchy, not the three-list system. Students may confuse the 1935 Act's provisions.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. UTs not represented in Rajya Sabha. 2. CEC adjudicates election disputes. 3. Parliament consists of Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha only.
questions.solution: All three statements are incorrect: UTs like Delhi and Puducherry ARE represented in Rajya Sabha (Statement 1 false); election disputes are adjudicated by courts, not CEC (Statement 2 false); Parliament includes President + Lok Sabha + Rajya Sabha (Statement 3 false). Students often think Parliament = two Houses only.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which emperor has been called Napoleon of India?
questions.solution: Samudragupta is called the 'Napoleon of India' by historian V.A. Smith because of his extensive military conquests across the subcontinent. The Allahabad Pillar inscription describes his campaigns. Students confuse him with Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: The first metal discovered by man was
questions.solution: Copper was the first metal used by humans, leading to the Chalcolithic (Copper) Age before the Bronze Age. Copper occurs naturally in metallic form and was easy to work with. Students sometimes guess Iron as it is most common today.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Who wrote Ramcharita?
questions.solution: Ramcharita was written by Sandhyakara Nandi in the 12th century CE, describing the victories of the Pala king Ramapala. It is a Sanskrit text. Students confuse it with Ramcharitmanas (Tulsidas) which is in Awadhi.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? I. Alexander invaded India in 326 BC. II. Ambhi of Taxila welcomed Alexander. III. Alexander died in 332 BC.
questions.optionA: Only I and III are correct.
questions.optionB: Only II and III are correct.
questions.optionD: Only I and II are correct.
questions.solution: Statements I and II are correct. Alexander died in 323 BC in Babylon, not 332 BC (Statement III is false). 332 BC was when Alexander conquered Egypt. Students must remember Alexander's death year precisely.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which organisation was set up to check the impact of Christian missionaries?
questions.solution: Arya Samaj, founded by Dayananda Saraswati in 1875, was specifically established to counter the influence of Christian missionaries and revive Vedic religion. Brahmo Samaj focused on monotheism, not countering Christianity. Students confuse the objectives of these reform movements.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Who was the first secretary of Brahmo Sabha?
questions.solution: Tarachand Chakraborty was the first secretary of the Brahmo Sabha founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy in 1828. Roy was the founder, not the secretary. Students often confuse the roles of founders and secretaries in reform organisations.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Baithun School, set up in 1849, was associated with
questions.solution: Baithun School (1849) was established in Bengal for the education of women. It was associated with reformers working for women's upliftment. Students may confuse it with schools for depressed classes established by other reformers.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. August Offer made in 1940. 2. Poona Pact between Gandhi and Ambedkar. 3. Third Round Table Conference held in 1932.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: August Offer 1940 (by Viceroy Linlithgow), Poona Pact 1932 (Gandhi-Ambedkar on separate electorates), Third RTC 1932. Students often doubt Statement 3, thinking there were only two RTCs.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. GOI Act 1919 introduced dyarchy in provinces. 2. Act provided for direct election to Council of State and Legislative Assembly.
questions.solution: Both statements are correct: The Montagu-Chelmsford Act 1919 introduced dyarchy in provinces (Statement 1) and provided for direct elections to the Central Legislature (Statement 2). Students may doubt Statement 2, thinking direct elections came only with the 1935 Act.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Raja Ram Mohan Roy — Father of Modern India. 2. Sayyid Ahmad Khan — Muslim communalism. 3. Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt threw bombs in Central Assembly 1929.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct: Ram Mohan Roy is called Father of Modern India; Sayyid Ahmad Khan's Aligarh Movement is seen as fuelling Muslim separatism; Bhagat Singh and BK Dutt threw bombs in the Central Assembly on April 8, 1929. Students doubt Statement 3 thinking it was in Parliament.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about the Revolt of 1857 are correct? 1. Started by BEIC sepoys. 2. Queen Victoria's proclamation 1858 led to complete integration of armies. 3. Major turning point in history.
questions.solution: Statements 1 and 3 are correct. Statement 2 is incorrect: Queen Victoria's proclamation (1858) transferred power to the Crown but did NOT lead to complete integration of British and Indian armies; they remained separate. Students assume the proclamation unified all aspects of administration.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Young Bengal Movement founded by H.V. Derozio. 2. Vidyasagar campaigned for widow remarriage. 3. Brahmo Samaj denounced polytheism and idol worship.
questions.solution: All three statements are correct. Derozio founded Young Bengal; Vidyasagar was instrumental in the Hindu Widow's Remarriage Act 1856; Brahmo Samaj (founded by Ram Mohan Roy) rejected idol worship and polytheism. Students may doubt Statement 3's specificity about Brahmo Samaj.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements are correct? 1. Chengiz Khan reached Indus during Iltutmish's reign. 2. Taimur crossed Indus during Muhammad bin Tughlaq's reign. 3. Vasco da Gama reached Kerala during Deva Raya II's reign.
questions.solution: Only Statement 1 is correct: Chengiz Khan pursued the Khwarezm prince to the Indus during Iltutmish's reign (1221). Statement 2 is wrong — Taimur invaded during Nasiruddin Mahmud Shah Tughluq's reign (1398). Statement 3 is wrong — Vasco da Gama arrived in 1498 during Saluva Narasimha's or early Tuluva period. Students conflate different medieval rulers.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about Alauddin Khilji's administration are correct? 1. Market control policy. 2. Silver tanka currency for price control. 3. First sultan to fix land revenue in cash. 4. Constructed Siri Fort.
questions.solution: Statements 1, 3, and 4 are correct. Statement 2 is incorrect — the silver tanka was introduced by Iltutmish, not Alauddin Khilji. Alauddin's price control used market superintendents and spies, not a new currency. Students attribute all Khilji reforms to Alauddin.

questions.subject: History
questions.question: Which statements about Emperor Akbar are correct? 1. Issued Mahzarnama. 2. Mansabdari system primarily determined salary. 3. Battle of Haldighati 1576 was decisive Mughal victory.
questions.solution: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. Statement 3 is partially wrong — Haldighati (1576) was NOT a decisive victory; Maharana Pratap escaped and continued resistance. Akbar's forces won the battle but couldn't capture Pratap. Students think any battle won by Mughals was 'decisive'.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: The Planning Commission was established in the year
questions.solution: The Planning Commission of India was established on March 15, 1950. It was replaced by NITI Aayog in 2015. Students confuse the year of independence (1947) with the establishment of planning institutions.

questions.subject: Others
questions.question: The very first five-year plan of India was based on the model of:
questions.solution: India's First Five Year Plan (1951-56) was based on the Harrod-Domar growth model focusing on savings and investment. The Mahalanobis model was used in the Second Five Year Plan. Students commonly confuse the two models.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following pair is not correctly matched?
questions.optionA: Total area of India - 3.28 million sq. km
questions.solution: India currently has 28 states (after Jammu & Kashmir was bifurcated into 2 UTs in 2019). The number 29 is outdated. All other options are correct. Students may not be updated on the reorganisation of J&K.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following rivers of India crosses the tropic of cancer twice?
questions.solution: The Mahi River in Gujarat/Rajasthan crosses the Tropic of Cancer twice due to its curved course. The Narmada and Chambal flow roughly parallel to the Tropic of Cancer but do not cross it twice. Students rarely know the specific course of the Mahi river.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statement(s) about the Indian desert is/are correct? (a) Lies towards western margins of Aravalli Hills (b) Rainfall below 150 mm per year (c) Luni is only large river (d) All the above
questions.optionA: Lies towards western margins of Aravalli Hills
questions.optionC: Luni is the only large river in this region
questions.solution: All three statements about the Thar (Indian) Desert are correct: it lies west of the Aravallis, receives less than 150 mm rainfall, and the Luni is the only significant river. Students may doubt the rainfall figure or the Aravalli direction.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The correct south to northward sequence of Zaskar, Dhauladhar, Ladakh, Karakoram ranges is:
questions.solution: From south to north: Dhauladhar (2) → Zaskar (1) → Ladakh (3) → Karakoram (4). This is the standard sequence of Himalayan ranges from the Shivaliks northward. Students often confuse Zaskar and Ladakh range positions.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statement is NOT correct about Indian geography?
questions.optionA: The Western Ghats are relatively high in their Northern region.
questions.optionB: The Anaimudi is the highest peak in the Western Ghats.
questions.optionC: Tapti river lies to the south of Satpura.
questions.optionD: The Narmada and the Tapti river valleys are said to be old rift valleys.
questions.solution: The Western Ghats are higher in the SOUTH (Anaimudi, 2695m) and lower in the north. Statement (a) says they are high in the northern region, which is incorrect. All other options are factually accurate. Students confuse north-south elevation patterns.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which statements about Barren Island volcano are correct? 1. Active volcano in Indian territory. 2. Lies ~140 km east of Great Nicobar. 3. Last eruption in 1991, inactive since.
questions.solution: Only Statement 1 is correct: Barren Island is the only confirmed active volcano in South Asia, located in the Andaman Sea. Statement 2 is wrong — it is about 135 km northeast of Port Blair, not east of Great Nicobar. Statement 3 is wrong — it has erupted multiple times after 1991, including in 2017 and 2020.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Toda Tribe is the inhabitant of:
questions.solution: The Toda tribe is an indigenous pastoral community inhabiting the Nilgiri Hills of Tamil Nadu. They are known for their distinct culture and circular thatched huts. Students may confuse them with tribes from central Indian hill ranges.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Assertion: Yamuna becomes a dead river between Delhi and Agra most of the year. Reason: Yamuna is a non-perennial river.
questions.optionA: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
questions.optionB: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
questions.solution: The Assertion is correct — Yamuna is effectively 'dead' (highly polluted, no fresh flow) between Delhi and Agra due to industrial effluents. The Reason is FALSE — Yamuna IS a perennial river fed by Himalayan glaciers. The real reason is pollution and water diversion, not non-perennial nature.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The Indian monsoon is indicated by seasonal displacement because of
questions.optionA: Differential temperature of land and sea
questions.solution: The Indian monsoon is driven by the differential heating of land and sea (land heats and cools faster than sea), creating pressure differences that cause seasonal wind reversal. This thermal gradient is the primary cause. Students may overcomplicate this with ITCZ theory.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Sargasso Sea is related to the Ocean, that ocean is –
questions.solution: The Sargasso Sea is a region of the North Atlantic Ocean, bounded by four ocean currents forming the North Atlantic Gyre. It is named after the Sargassum seaweed that floats on its surface. Students confuse it with the Pacific Ocean.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which of the following is a warm current?
questions.solution: The Brazil Current is a warm ocean current flowing southward along the eastern coast of South America. Falkland and Labrador are cold currents; Canary is also a cold current off NW Africa. Students often confuse Atlantic Ocean currents.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Which strait connects the Red Sea and the Indian Ocean?
questions.solution: The Strait of Bab-el-Mandeb connects the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean. The Strait of Hormuz connects the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman. Students often confuse these two strategically important straits.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: 'Great Barrier Reef' is—
questions.optionD: A range of the Rocky Mountains
questions.solution: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, located off the coast of Queensland, Australia. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Options (b), (c), (d) are obviously incorrect distractors.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: Arrange atmospheric zones in increasing order of distance from earth's surface: 1.Thermosphere 2.Troposphere 4.Mesosphere 3.Stratosphere
questions.solution: The correct order from Earth's surface (increasing distance): Troposphere(2) → Stratosphere(3) → Mesosphere(4) → Thermosphere(1). The mnemonic 'The Stratosphere Meets Thermos' helps remember. Students often place Mesosphere and Thermosphere in wrong order.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: A sudden fall of Mercury in Barometer indicates;
questions.solution: A sudden fall in barometric pressure (mercury falling rapidly) indicates an approaching storm or bad weather. High pressure indicates fair weather; low and falling pressure signals storms. Students confuse gradual fall (rain) with sudden fall (storm).

questions.subject: Geography
questions.question: The headquarter of the International Maritime Organization is located at:
questions.solution: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is headquartered in London, UK. Geneva hosts WHO, WTO, and other UN bodies. Students confuse London-based international organisations with Geneva-based ones.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following will contain same number of atoms as 20 g of calcium?
questions.solution: 20 g Ca = \(\frac{20}{40}\) = 0.5 mol Ca = 0.5 mol atoms. 12 g C = \(\frac{12}{12}\) = 1 mol C? No — 0.5 mol Ca needs 0.5 mol atoms of another element. \(\frac{12}{12}\times N_A = N_A\) atoms (1 mol), not same. Actually 20/40 = 0.5 mol Ca. For same atoms: 0.5 mol C = 6 g, 0.5 mol Mg = 12 g. Answer is (b) 12 g of carbon (0.5 mol × 12 = 6g)... re-examining: 12g carbon = 1 mol = \(6.02\times10^{23}\) atoms; 20g Ca = 0.5 mol = \(3.01\times10^{23}\) atoms. The matching option should give 0.5 mol. 24g Mg = 1 mol Mg atoms ≠. 12g C = 1 mol ≠. Students must equate moles of atoms correctly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A device used for measurement of radioactivity is
questions.solution: The Geiger-Müller (G.M.) counter detects and measures ionising radiation (alpha, beta, gamma). A mass spectrometer separates ions by mass; a cyclotron accelerates particles. Students may confuse instruments used in nuclear physics.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Molten sodium chloride conducts electricity due to the presence of
questions.solution: Molten NaCl dissociates into Na\(^+\) and Cl\(^-\) ions which carry electrical charge. Free electrons conduct electricity in metals, not ionic compounds. Students confuse metallic conduction (electrons) with electrolytic conduction (ions).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum appears in
questions.solution: The Balmer series corresponds to electron transitions to n=2 level in hydrogen, emitting visible light (wavelengths 400–700 nm). Lyman series is UV; Paschen/Brackett are infrared. Students confuse the series with their spectral regions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Rutherford's experiment, when thin metal foils were bombarded with \(\alpha\)-particles:
questions.optionA: Most alpha particles pass through without deflection
questions.optionB: All alpha particles pass through the foil
questions.optionC: Most alpha particles are deflected through small angles
questions.optionD: Most alpha particles are deflected back
questions.solution: Most \(\alpha\)-particles passed straight through because the atom is mostly empty space. Only a few were deflected at large angles due to the dense, positive nucleus. Students confuse the result with the conclusion (nuclear model) and think most are deflected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Select the correct statement from the following
questions.optionA: Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin
questions.optionB: Brass is an alloy of copper and aluminium
questions.optionD: Stainless steel is chromium plated iron
questions.solution: Bronze = Cu + Sn (copper and tin). Brass = Cu + Zn (copper and zinc, not aluminium). German silver contains Cu, Zn, Ni — no actual silver. Stainless steel is an alloy (Fe + Cr + Ni), not chromium plated. Students confuse brass and bronze compositions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Electrical conductivity of metal decreases
questions.solution: In metals, conductivity decreases with increasing temperature because lattice vibrations increase, causing more collisions with electrons. This is opposite to semiconductors (whose conductivity increases with temperature). Students confuse metal and semiconductor behaviour.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kirchhoff's current law is based on
questions.solution: KCL states that the sum of currents entering a node equals the sum leaving it, which is based on conservation of electric charge. KVL (voltage law) is based on conservation of energy. Students swap the bases of KCL and KVL.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The dimensions of heat capacity are
questions.solution: Heat capacity = \(\frac{\text{Heat energy}}{\text{Temperature}}\). Heat energy has dimensions \([ML^2T^{-2}]\) and temperature has dimension \([K]\). So heat capacity = \([ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]\). Students often forget to take the reciprocal of K.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A black body is at 500 K. It emits energy at a rate proportional to
questions.solution: By Stefan-Boltzmann law, power emitted by a black body \(E \propto T^4\). At T = 500 K, \(E \propto (500)^4\). Students sometimes confuse with Wien's law or think the power is proportional to T only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: \(n\) equal resistors are first connected in series and then connected in parallel. What is the ratio of the maximum resistance to the minimum resistance?
questions.solution: Series resistance \(R_s = nR\) (maximum); Parallel resistance \(R_p = \frac{R}{n}\) (minimum). Ratio = \(\frac{nR}{R/n} = n^2\). Students forget to divide both and often calculate just nR/R = n.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The ratio of the dimension of Planck's constant and that of moment of inertia is the dimension of
questions.solution: Planck's constant \(h\) has dimensions \([ML^2T^{-1}]\). Moment of inertia has dimensions \([ML^2]\). Ratio = \(\frac{[ML^2T^{-1}]}{[ML^2]} = [T^{-1}]\) = frequency. Students may guess velocity by intuition without computing dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass 2 gm has charge \(2\mu C\). Through what potential difference must it be accelerated from rest to acquire speed of 10 m/s?
questions.solution: Work-energy theorem: \(qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\). So \(V = \frac{mv^2}{2q} = \frac{(2\times10^{-3})(10)^2}{2\times(2\times10^{-6})} = \frac{0.2}{4\times10^{-6}} = 50000\) V = 50 kV. The correct answer should be (c) 50 kV. Students must be careful with unit conversions (gm to kg, \(\mu C\) to C).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three resistances each of \(4\Omega\) are connected to form a triangle. The resistance between any two terminals is
questions.solution: Between any two terminals: one arm (\(4\Omega\)) is in parallel with two arms in series (\(8\Omega\)). Equivalent resistance = \(\frac{4\times8}{4+8} = \frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3}\Omega\). Students forget that two sides are in series and that combination is in parallel with the third side.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem is based upon
questions.solution: Bernoulli's theorem is derived from the work-energy theorem and represents conservation of energy in fluid flow (pressure energy + kinetic energy + potential energy = constant). Continuity equation is based on mass conservation. Students sometimes say momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An iron needle floats on the surface of water. This phenomenon is attributed to:
questions.solution: A needle floats due to surface tension — the water surface behaves like an elastic membrane supporting the needle. Upthrust (buoyancy) would be insufficient for a denser-than-water object. Students often guess upthrust thinking about Archimedes' principle.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Chemical formula of borax (Sodium tetraborate) is
questions.solution: Borax is \(Na_2B_4O_7 \cdot 10H_2O\) (sodium tetraborate decahydrate). It contains 10 molecules of water of crystallisation. Students confuse it with washing soda (\(Na_2CO_3 \cdot 10H_2O\)) or confuse the number of water molecules.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A moving body is covering distances in proportion to the square of time. The acceleration of the body is
questions.solution: If \(s \propto t^2\), then \(s = kt^2\) for some constant k. Differentiating: \(v = 2kt\) and \(a = 2k\) = constant. This is the equation of uniformly accelerated motion. Students may think acceleration increases because velocity increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A convex lens has focal length \(f\). It makes a virtual image of size \(n\) times the object. The distance of the object from the lens is
questions.solution: For virtual image by convex lens: magnification \(m = n = \frac{v}{u}\) and lens formula \(\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u}\). Using \(v = nu\): \(\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{nu} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1-n}{nu}\), so \(u = \frac{(1-n)f}{n} = \frac{(n-1)}{n}f\) (taking magnitude). Students confuse sign conventions for virtual images.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The purest form of Fe is
questions.solution: Wrought iron is the purest form of iron with carbon content less than 0.05%. Cast iron has 2-4% carbon; steel has 0.2-2% carbon; stainless steel contains chromium and nickel. Students confuse purity with strength.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to which law CO and \(CO_2\) are formed?
questions.solution: The Law of Multiple Proportions states that when two elements (C and O) combine to form more than one compound (CO and \(CO_2\)), the masses of one element that combine with a fixed mass of the other are in simple whole number ratios (1:2 for oxygen). Students confuse this with the law of constant proportions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The neutron was discovered by.
questions.solution: The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932. Rutherford discovered the proton; Goldstein discovered canal rays (protons); Moseley worked on atomic numbers and X-ray spectra. Students confuse the discoverers of subatomic particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following oxides is acidic?
questions.solution: \(SO_2\) is an acidic oxide (dissolves in water to form \(H_2SO_3\)). \(Na_2O\) is a basic oxide. \(Al_2O_3\) is amphoteric (both acidic and basic). Students may choose \(Al_2O_3\) because it can show acidic properties, but it is primarily amphoteric.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The large energy released in atomic bomb explosion is due to
questions.optionC: conversion of heavy atoms to lighter atoms
questions.solution: The energy released in nuclear fission is due to mass defect — a small amount of mass (\(\Delta m\)) is converted to energy as per \(E = \Delta mc^2\). This 'appreciable loss of mass' results in enormous energy. Students think it is due to neutron release alone.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is amphoteric?
questions.solution: \(ZnO\) is amphoteric — it reacts with both acids (\(ZnO + H_2SO_4 \to ZnSO_4 + H_2O\)) and bases (\(ZnO + NaOH \to Na_2ZnO_2 + H_2O\)). \(Li_2O\) is basic; \(SO_2\) is acidic. Students may not know ZnO's amphoteric nature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The energy equivalent to 1 kilogram of matter is about
questions.solution: By \(E = mc^2\): \(E = 1 \times (3\times10^8)^2 = 9\times10^{16} \approx 10^{17}\) joule. The speed of light \(c = 3\times10^8\) m/s. Students forget to square c and underestimate the result.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following phenomena cannot take place in sound waves?
questions.solution: Polarisation can only occur in transverse waves (like light). Sound waves are longitudinal (compressions and rarefactions) and CANNOT be polarised. Reflection, interference, and diffraction are exhibited by both transverse and longitudinal waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The number of electrons contained in 1 coulomb of charge is
questions.solution: Charge of one electron \(e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\) C. Number of electrons in 1 C = \(\frac{1}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = 6.25 \times 10^{18}\). Students confuse the value of e with its reciprocal and misplace the power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Refractive indices of glass and water w.r.t. air are \(\frac{3}{2}\) and \(\frac{4}{3}\) respectively. The refractive index of glass w.r.t. water will be
questions.solution: \(n_{gw} = \frac{n_{ga}}{n_{wa}} = \frac{3/2}{4/3} = \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{9}{8}\). The refractive index of glass with respect to water = \(\frac{9}{8}\). Students often invert the ratio or confuse which value goes in the numerator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a monochromatic light passes from vacuum to a material, which characteristic does NOT change?
questions.solution: Frequency of light does NOT change when it passes from one medium to another. Velocity (\(v = c/n\)) and wavelength (\(\lambda = \lambda_0/n\)) both change with medium. Students confuse wavelength and frequency changes in different media.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two wires of same metal, same length, cross-sections in ratio 3:1, joined in series. Resistance of thicker wire = \(10\Omega\). Total resistance of combination:
questions.solution: Resistance \(R \propto \frac{1}{A}\). Thicker wire (area 3A): \(R_1 = 10\Omega\). Thinner wire (area A): \(R_2 = 10 \times 3 = 30\Omega\). Total in series: \(R = 10 + 30 = 40\Omega\). Students use ratio in wrong direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The time period of a simple pendulum is 2 seconds. If its length is increased by 4 times, then its period becomes
questions.solution: \(T \propto \sqrt{L}\). If L is increased 4 times: \(T' = T\sqrt{4} = 2 \times 2 = 4\) seconds. Students sometimes think T increases by 4 (getting 8 s) by forgetting the square root relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The temperature at which speed of sound becomes double that at 27°C is
questions.solution: Speed of sound \(v \propto \sqrt{T}\) (in Kelvin). At 27°C = 300 K. For double speed: \(\frac{v'}{v} = \sqrt{\frac{T'}{300}} = 2\), so \(T' = 4 \times 300 = 1200\) K = 927°C. Students forget to convert to Kelvin and work directly in Celsius.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At 0 K, which of the following properties of a gas will be zero?
questions.solution: At absolute zero (0 K), all molecular motion ceases and the kinetic energy of gas molecules becomes zero. Mass and density do not become zero; potential energy depends on intermolecular forces, not temperature alone. Students confuse 0°C with 0 K.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Time taken by sunlight to pass through a glass window of thickness 4 mm with refractive index \(\frac{3}{2}\)
questions.solution: Speed in glass \(v = \frac{c}{n} = \frac{3\times10^8}{1.5} = 2\times10^8\) m/s. Time \(t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{4\times10^{-3}}{2\times10^8} = 2\times10^{-11}\) s. Students forget to divide c by n and use c directly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A 2 kg and 4 kg ball are dropped from 60 feet. After falling 30 feet, their respective kinetic energies ratio:
questions.solution: KE = mgh (same height h = 30 ft for both). KE\(_1 = 2gh\), KE\(_2 = 4gh\). Ratio = \(\frac{2}{4} = 1:2\). Both experience same acceleration (g), so ratio of KE = ratio of masses. Students may think heavier ball falls faster.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Superconductivity observed at 95K. This temperature is approximately equivalent to
questions.solution: 95 K = 95 - 273 = -178°C. To convert to Fahrenheit: \(F = -178 \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 = -320.4 + 32 = -288.4°F \approx -288°F\). Actually option (c) -288°F matches better. Rechecking: 95K = -178°C = (-178×9/5)+32 = -288.4°F ≈ -288°F. Students must use the correct conversion formula.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a wire is stretched and its radius becomes \(\frac{r}{2}\), its resistance will be
questions.solution: Volume constant: \(\pi r^2 L = \pi (r/2)^2 L'\) → \(L' = 4L\). New resistance \(R' = \rho \frac{L'}{A'} = \rho \frac{4L}{\pi(r/2)^2} = \rho \frac{4L}{\pi r^2/4} = 16\rho\frac{L}{\pi r^2} = 16R\). Students forget volume is conserved when stretching.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The temperature of sun can be found out by using
questions.solution: Wien's Displacement Law (\(\lambda_{max} T = b\)) relates the peak wavelength of emitted radiation to temperature, allowing calculation of the Sun's surface temperature from its emission spectrum. Stefan-Boltzmann law gives total power. Students confuse the two laws' applications.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following statements is NOT true?
questions.optionA: Resistance of intrinsic semiconductors decreases with increase of temperature.
questions.optionB: Doping pure Si with trivalent impurity gives p-type semiconductors.
questions.optionC: The majority carriers in n-type semiconductors are holes.
questions.optionD: A p-n junction can act as a semiconductor diode.
questions.solution: In n-type semiconductors, the majority carriers are ELECTRONS (not holes). Holes are majority carriers in p-type semiconductors. Statement (c) is false and is the answer. Statements (a), (b), (d) are all correct facts about semiconductors.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The vitamins, which are generally excreted in urine are
questions.solution: Water-soluble vitamins (B-complex and C) are excreted in urine when in excess. Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in body fat and liver. Students confuse fat-soluble with water-soluble vitamins.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Deficiency of which elements is responsible for weakening of bones? 1.Calcium 2.Phosphorus 3.Nitrogen 4.Carbon
questions.solution: Calcium and Phosphorus are the primary minerals in bones (as hydroxyapatite). Deficiency of either weakens bones. Nitrogen and Carbon are structural elements in organic molecules but not specifically responsible for bone weakness. Students may include more elements than necessary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one among the following animal tissues transports hormones and maintains water balance?
questions.solution: Connective tissue, specifically blood (a fluid connective tissue), transports hormones and helps maintain water balance (osmotic balance). Areolar tissue is loose connective tissue providing support. Students may confuse blood with nervous tissue for transport.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following structures of a plant is responsible for transpiration?
questions.solution: Transpiration (loss of water vapour from plants) primarily occurs through stomata on leaves. Xylem transports water from roots; phloem transports food. Students confuse the structure for water transport (xylem) with that for transpiration (stomata).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Cinnabar is an ore of
questions.solution: Cinnabar (HgS) is the principal ore of mercury (Hg). Copper ore includes chalcopyrite; nickel comes from pentlandite; tungsten from wolframite. Students confuse cinnabar with copper ores due to its reddish colour.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Wavelengths of visible light \(\lambda_v\), X-rays \(\lambda_x\) and microwaves \(\lambda_m\): correct order is
questions.solution: In the EM spectrum: microwaves have the longest wavelength (\(\sim\)mm–cm), visible light intermediate (\(\sim\)400–700 nm), X-rays very short (\(\sim\)0.01–10 nm). So \(\lambda_m > \lambda_v > \lambda_x\). Students may misplace visible light and X-rays in the spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An object weighs 10N in air. When fully immersed in liquid, it weighs 8N. The weight of liquid displaced is
questions.solution: By Archimedes' principle, buoyant force = weight in air − weight in liquid = 10 − 8 = 2N. The buoyant force equals the weight of liquid displaced = 2N. Students sometimes calculate the weight of liquid displaced as the weight in liquid (8N).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Source of sun's energy is
questions.solution: The sun produces energy through nuclear fusion — hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium, releasing enormous energy (\(4H \to He + energy\)). Fission (splitting heavy nuclei) is used in nuclear reactors and atomic bombs, not in stars. Students confuse fusion and fission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two resistances of \(10\Omega\) are parallelly connected. Equivalent resistance will be
questions.solution: For two equal resistors R in parallel: \(R_{eq} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5\Omega\). Students may add them (series) or use the wrong formula. The parallel formula is \(\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: "Good emitters are good absorbers." This statement is based on
questions.solution: Kirchhoff's Law of radiation states that at thermal equilibrium, good emitters are also good absorbers of radiation. Stefan's law gives total radiated power; Newton's law relates cooling rate to temperature difference. Students confuse Kirchhoff's radiation law with his electrical circuit laws.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is true according to the author?
[Passage I: About agricultural societies and civilizations]
questions.optionA: Agriculture has always been part of all civilizations.
questions.optionB: Not all civilizations have undergone the processes of agriculture.
questions.optionC: Agriculture gave birth to new civilizations.
questions.optionD: Communities discontinued agriculture to become civilizations.
questions.solution: The passage states 'no civilization can become one without passing through the stage of agriculture', implying agriculture gave birth to civilizations. Option A is false (not all agricultural societies become civilizations); Option B contradicts the passage directly. Students confuse A and C by misreading 'not all agricultural societies become civilizations' as meaning civilizations don't need agriculture.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
A significant number of people were sent to carry out other work from agriculture because
[Passage I: About agricultural societies and civilizations]
questions.optionB: people were needed to build monuments, weapons, jewellery, etc.
questions.optionD: this enabled the development of civilizations.
questions.solution: The passage states 'as productivity improves, not all people would need to be engaged in producing or procuring food' — implying sufficient agricultural products freed people for other tasks. Option B lists specific activities (monuments, weapons) but the root cause is sufficient productivity. Students often choose B as it matches specific examples in the passage, missing the causal explanation.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
What kind of agriculture based societies would emerge as civilizations?
[Passage I: About agricultural societies and civilizations]
questions.optionA: Societies which achieved high productivity in agriculture had the opportunity to find time for other work.
questions.optionB: Societies which depended on agriculture completely moved to other fruitful work so as to move to many places.
questions.optionC: Societies which transitioned from one stage of agriculture to another.
questions.optionD: Societies which could not do agriculture for lack of resources moved to other work.
questions.solution: The passage explains that high-productivity agriculture frees people for other tasks, which is the prerequisite for civilization. The key phrase is 'transitioned to high-productivity agriculture'. Students pick C because 'transitioned' appears in the passage, but transitioning stages of agriculture is not the same as achieving high productivity.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
People as groups were convinced to do new work through
[Passage I: About agricultural societies and civilizations]
questions.solution: The passage states 'creating new ideologies and new hierarchies or power structures to coerce or otherwise convince large groups of people to devote their time to new tasks for very little reward.' This directly matches option B. Students choose A (reward, force) but the passage specifically mentions 'very little reward' — the convincing was done through ideology and power, not rewards.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Which word in the passage means 'changeover'?
[Passage I: About agricultural societies and civilizations]
questions.solution: 'Changeover' means a change from one system or activity to another. 'Transitioned' is used in the passage to mean a shift/changeover to high-productivity agriculture. 'Channel' means to direct; 'Coerce' means to force; 'Hierarchies' means ranked structures. Students confuse 'Channel' (B) with changeover as both involve movement or redirection.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
According to the author, which one of the following statements is NOT true?
[Passage II: About reading habits and preconceptions]
questions.optionB: Readers have expectations when they read an article or a book.
questions.optionC: Readers look for specific information in any of their readings.
questions.optionD: Readers assume that everything they read will have new information.
questions.solution: The passage says readers come with preconceptions (A true), have expectations (B true), and look for specific information (C true). Nowhere does the passage say readers assume everything will have new information — in fact, 'we probably know a little about the book or article we are reading even before we start'. Students often miss D as it is a subtle negative inference.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Our expectations and predispositions may, however, blind us because
[Passage II: About reading habits and preconceptions]
questions.optionA: we may not get the actual ideas of the author.
questions.optionB: we will get the actual ideas of the author.
questions.optionC: we may disagree with the author.
questions.optionD: we will agree with all the ideas of the author.
questions.solution: The passage states our expectations 'blind us to what the article and its author is actually saying' — meaning we miss the actual ideas. The word 'blind' implies we may not perceive the author's real message. Students choose C (disagree) but the passage doesn't say disagreement — it says we see only what we expect, not what is actually there.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
One of the ways to improve our reading habits is to
[Passage II: About reading habits and preconceptions]
questions.optionA: break the routine by changing the time of reading.
questions.optionB: change the types of topics we read.
questions.optionC: break the routine of reading the same newspaper.
questions.optionD: stop reading for some time and then restart reading.
questions.solution: The passage explicitly says 'we expose ourselves to the same newspaper, the same magazine, even books by authors with the same perspectives' and advocates breaking this routine. Option C directly captures this. Students pick B (change topics) but the passage emphasizes breaking the routine of same sources, not just changing topics.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Which quality does the author here advocate, to be a good reader?
[Passage II: About reading habits and preconceptions]
questions.optionA: Being objective to the ideas of the author
questions.solution: The author warns against preconceptions blinding readers and advocates stepping back to 'reflect on our reading habits' — which means being objective. Option B is what readers should avoid. Option C is explicitly cautioned against. Students sometimes choose D (disagreeing) but the passage advocates objectivity, not disagreement.

questions.subject: English
questions.question:
Which word in the passage means 'viewpoints'?
[Passage II: About reading habits and preconceptions]
questions.solution: 'Viewpoints' means particular attitudes or ways of considering something. 'Perspectives' is the exact synonym used in the passage in 'books by authors with the same perspectives.' 'Preconceptions' means pre-formed opinions; 'Predispositions' means tendencies; 'Pattern' means a repeated arrangement. Students confuse 'Preconceptions' (A) with viewpoints.

questions.subject: English
questions.question: After mysteriously expanding for decades, (a) Antarctica's sea ice cover (b) starting melting. (c) No error (d)
